content.newText:
--- tags: [lecture] course: Computer-Vision source: 12_feature_detectors2.pdf lecture: 12 created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 12 — Feature detectors 2 ## Overview Lecture 11 covered edges and boundaries. Lecture 12 moves up to **richer, sparser features** — the kind you can match across images for stitching, panorama, alignment and structure-from-motion. Three layers: 1. **Corner detection** — local windows where intensity changes in *two* independent directions; quantified by the eigenvalues of the gradient covariance matrix; **Harris** is the canonical detector. 2. **SIFT-style scale-invariant blob keypoints** — detect with scale-normalized [[laplacian-of-gaussian|LoG]] (or the cheaper [[difference-of-gaussian|DoG]] approximation), describe with **histograms of gradient orientations** in 4×4 tiles. 3. **Alignment algorithms** — match descriptors across images (with [[non-maximum-suppression]] and **RANSAC** for outlier rejection), then estimate the geometric transform (Kabsch for rigid body) or run **ICP** when correspondences are unknown. ## Key concepts - [[corner-detection]] — what a corner is, gradient-cloud picture - [[structure-tensor]] — gradient covariance, eigenvalues = principal axes - [[harris-corner-detector]] — closed-form response avoiding eigenvalue computation - [[sift-keypoint-detection]] — scale-space LoG/DoG extrema - [[difference-of-gaussian]] — cheap approximation to scale-normalized LoG - [[sift-descriptor]] — 4×4 grid of gradient orientation histograms, 128-D - [[feature-matching-ransac]] — brute-force / FLANN matching + RANSAC outlier rejection - [[kabsch-algorithm]] — closed-form rigid-body alignment via SVD - [[icp-algorithm]] — iterative alignment when correspondences are unknown - [[laplacian-of-gaussian]] — extended: scale-normalised blob detection at $\sigma \approx r_\text{blob}$ ## Equations Weighted centroid of a gradient cloud (slide 8): $$\mu = (\bar{x}, \bar{y}) = \sum_i \frac{1}{w_i}\!\left(\sum_i w_i\, x_i,\; \sum_i w_i\, y_i\right)$$ Weighted gradient covariance / structure tensor: $$C = \sum_i \frac{1}{w_i} \sum_i w_i \begin{pmatrix} x_i - \bar{x} \\ y_i - \bar{y} \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x_i - \bar{x} \\ y_i - \bar{y} \end{pmatrix}^\top$$ (In practice: $x_i = I_x$, $y_i = I_y$; the eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ are the principal axes of the gradient ellipse.) Harris response (slide 12): $$R = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2, \qquad k \approx 0.05$$ Equivalently $R = \det(C) - k\,\mathrm{tr}(C)^2$ — avoids explicit eigendecomposition. Scale-normalized Laplacian of Gaussian (slide 16): $$\sigma^2 \nabla^2 G(x, \sigma) * f(x)$$ DoG ≈ scale-normalized LoG (slide 18): $$G(x, s\sigma) - G(x, \sigma) \approx (s - 1)\sigma^2 \nabla^2 G(x, \sigma)$$ SIFT descriptor distance metrics (slide 31): $$d(H_1, H_2) = \sqrt{\sum_k (H_1(k) - H_2(k))^2}$$ $$\mathrm{nCC}(H_1, H_2) = \frac{\sum_k (H_1(k) - \overline{H_1})(H_2(k) - \overline{H_2})}{\sqrt{\sum_k (H_1(k) - \overline{H_1})^2}\,\sqrt{\sum_k (H_2(k) - \overline{H_2})^2}}$$ Kabsch alignment problem (slide 38): $$R^*, t^* = \arg\min_{R, t} \sum_i \| R\,x_i + t - y_i \|^2$$ Algorithm: subtract centroids → covariance $H = \sum_i \tilde{x}_i \tilde{y}_i^\top$ → SVD $H = U\Sigma V^\top$ → $R = V U^\top$ → $t = \bar{y} - R\bar{x}$. ## Diagrams ### Local-region taxonomy from gradient distribution (slides 3–11) ```mermaid flowchart TD win["window over image"] --> grads["gradients in window
scatter (I_x, I_y)"] grads --> shape{"shape of cloud?"} shape -->|"compact blob
λ1, λ2 small"| flat["flat region
(no structure)"] shape -->|"elongated ellipse
λ_max ≫ λ_min"| edge["edge
(one strong gradient direction)"] shape -->|"isotropic, both large
λ1 ≈ λ2 large"| corner["corner
(two independent gradients)"] ``` ### Harris response surface (slide 11) | Region | $\lambda_1$ | $\lambda_2$ | Harris $R$ | |---|---|---|---| | Flat | small | small | small (near 0) | | Edge | large | small (or vice-versa) | large negative | | Corner | large | large | large positive | ### SIFT keypoint detection pipeline (slides 16–21) ```mermaid flowchart TD img["input image"] --> pyr["build Gaussian pyramid
scales σ, kσ, k²σ, ..."] pyr --> dog["adjacent-scale DoG
D = G(σ_i+1) − G(σ_i)
≈ (k−1)σ² ∇²G"] dog --> stack["3D stack:
(x, y, σ)"] stack --> ext["3×3×3 local extrema
across space + scale"] ext --> thr["threshold weak responses"] thr --> kp["keypoints with (x, y, σ, dominant θ)"] ``` ### SIFT descriptor construction (slides 23–30) ```mermaid flowchart LR kp["keypoint
(x, y, σ)"] --> patch["extract patch,
rescale by 1/σ"] patch --> rot["rotate so dominant
orientation aligns with axis"] rot --> grid["4×4 spatial grid"] grid --> hist["per cell:
orientation histogram
(8 bins)"] hist --> concat["concatenate 16 × 8 = 128 numbers"] concat --> desc["SIFT descriptor"] ``` ### Why histograms of orientations beat raw patches (slide 24–25) | Property | Raw normalized patch | HoG-style descriptor | |---|---|---| | Sensitive to small misalignment | yes | no (binning absorbs it) | | Sensitive to interpolation artefacts | yes | no | | Sensitive to additive brightness | partly | no (orientations only) | | Sensitive to contrast scaling | yes | mostly invariant | | Dimensionality | high (raw pixels) | 128 | ### Feature matching pipeline (slides 32–35) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["image 1 SIFT descriptors {d_i}"] --> match["pairwise nearest-neighbour
(BFMatcher / FLANN ANN)"] B["image 2 SIFT descriptors {d_j}"] --> match match --> raw["candidate matches
(many false positives)"] raw --> ransac["RANSAC
fit a transform model
keep inliers only"] ransac --> clean["clean correspondences"] clean --> kab["Kabsch / least-squares
final transform"] ``` ### Geometric alignment decision tree (slides 36–40) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["two point clouds X, Y"] --> q1{"correspondences known?"} q1 -->|"yes"| kab["Kabsch (rigid),
or affine / homography LS"] q1 -->|"no, but roughly aligned"| icp["ICP
nearest neighbour ↔ Kabsch loop"] q1 -->|"no, totally unaligned"| ransac["RANSAC over random pairs
then refine with ICP"] ``` ### ICP loop (slide 40) ```mermaid flowchart LR init["X (source), Y (target),
initial guess"] --> nn["nearest_neighbor(X, Y)
→ correspondences"] nn --> kab["Kabsch on corresponded pairs
→ R, t"] kab --> apply["X ← R · X + t"] apply --> conv{"converged?"} conv -->|"no"| nn conv -->|"yes"| done["aligned X"] ``` ## Open questions - Why exactly $k \approx 0.05$ in Harris? (Empirical sweet spot — but is there theory?) - For SIFT, the $\sigma^2$ normalization is what makes responses comparable across scales. What goes wrong if you forget it? - ICP's failure mode is converging to a wrong local minimum. How does the "roughly aligned" assumption interact with the basin of attraction? ## Connections to other lectures - Builds on [[laplacian-of-gaussian]] (lecture 11) — but used here as a *blob* detector across scales rather than an edge detector at one scale. - [[non-maximum-suppression]] (lecture 11) reappears for SIFT keypoint thresholding. - [[image-gradient]] underlies both Harris (covariance of gradients) and SIFT (orientation histograms). - [[derivative-of-gaussian]] is what pre-computes the gradients used here.

rawInput.content:
--- tags: [lecture] course: Computer-Vision source: 12_feature_detectors2.pdf lecture: 12 created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 12 — Feature detectors 2 ## Overview Lecture 11 covered edges and boundaries. Lecture 12 moves up to **richer, sparser features** — the kind you can match across images for stitching, panorama, alignment and structure-from-motion. Three layers: 1. **Corner detection** — local windows where intensity changes in *two* independent directions; quantified by the eigenvalues of the gradient covariance matrix; **Harris** is the canonical detector. 2. **SIFT-style scale-invariant blob keypoints** — detect with scale-normalized [[laplacian-of-gaussian|LoG]] (or the cheaper [[difference-of-gaussian|DoG]] approximation), describe with **histograms of gradient orientations** in 4×4 tiles. 3. **Alignment algorithms** — match descriptors across images (with [[non-maximum-suppression]] and **RANSAC** for outlier rejection), then estimate the geometric transform (Kabsch for rigid body) or run **ICP** when correspondences are unknown. ## Key concepts - [[corner-detection]] — what a corner is, gradient-cloud picture - [[structure-tensor]] — gradient covariance, eigenvalues = principal axes - [[harris-corner-detector]] — closed-form response avoiding eigenvalue computation - [[sift-keypoint-detection]] — scale-space LoG/DoG extrema - [[difference-of-gaussian]] — cheap approximation to scale-normalized LoG - [[sift-descriptor]] — 4×4 grid of gradient orientation histograms, 128-D - [[feature-matching-ransac]] — brute-force / FLANN matching + RANSAC outlier rejection - [[kabsch-algorithm]] — closed-form rigid-body alignment via SVD - [[icp-algorithm]] — iterative alignment when correspondences are unknown - [[laplacian-of-gaussian]] — extended: scale-normalised blob detection at $\sigma \approx r_\text{blob}$ ## Equations Weighted centroid of a gradient cloud (slide 8): $$\mu = (\bar{x}, \bar{y}) = \sum_i \frac{1}{w_i}\!\left(\sum_i w_i\, x_i,\; \sum_i w_i\, y_i\right)$$ Weighted gradient covariance / structure tensor: $$C = \sum_i \frac{1}{w_i} \sum_i w_i \begin{pmatrix} x_i - \bar{x} \\ y_i - \bar{y} \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x_i - \bar{x} \\ y_i - \bar{y} \end{pmatrix}^\top$$ (In practice: $x_i = I_x$, $y_i = I_y$; the eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ are the principal axes of the gradient ellipse.) Harris response (slide 12): $$R = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2, \qquad k \approx 0.05$$ Equivalently $R = \det(C) - k\,\mathrm{tr}(C)^2$ — avoids explicit eigendecomposition. Scale-normalized Laplacian of Gaussian (slide 16): $$\sigma^2 \nabla^2 G(x, \sigma) * f(x)$$ DoG ≈ scale-normalized LoG (slide 18): $$G(x, s\sigma) - G(x, \sigma) \approx (s - 1)\sigma^2 \nabla^2 G(x, \sigma)$$ SIFT descriptor distance metrics (slide 31): $$d(H_1, H_2) = \sqrt{\sum_k (H_1(k) - H_2(k))^2}$$ $$\mathrm{nCC}(H_1, H_2) = \frac{\sum_k (H_1(k) - \overline{H_1})(H_2(k) - \overline{H_2})}{\sqrt{\sum_k (H_1(k) - \overline{H_1})^2}\,\sqrt{\sum_k (H_2(k) - \overline{H_2})^2}}$$ Kabsch alignment problem (slide 38): $$R^*, t^* = \arg\min_{R, t} \sum_i \| R\,x_i + t - y_i \|^2$$ Algorithm: subtract centroids → covariance $H = \sum_i \tilde{x}_i \tilde{y}_i^\top$ → SVD $H = U\Sigma V^\top$ → $R = V U^\top$ → $t = \bar{y} - R\bar{x}$. ## Diagrams ### Local-region taxonomy from gradient distribution (slides 3–11) ```mermaid flowchart TD win["window over image"] --> grads["gradients in window
scatter (I_x, I_y)"] grads --> shape{"shape of cloud?"} shape -->|"compact blob
λ1, λ2 small"| flat["flat region
(no structure)"] shape -->|"elongated ellipse
λ_max ≫ λ_min"| edge["edge
(one strong gradient direction)"] shape -->|"isotropic, both large
λ1 ≈ λ2 large"| corner["corner
(two independent gradients)"] ``` ### Harris response surface (slide 11) | Region | $\lambda_1$ | $\lambda_2$ | Harris $R$ | |---|---|---|---| | Flat | small | small | small (near 0) | | Edge | large | small (or vice-versa) | large negative | | Corner | large | large | large positive | ### SIFT keypoint detection pipeline (slides 16–21) ```mermaid flowchart TD img["input image"] --> pyr["build Gaussian pyramid
scales σ, kσ, k²σ, ..."] pyr --> dog["adjacent-scale DoG
D = G(σ_i+1) − G(σ_i)
≈ (k−1)σ² ∇²G"] dog --> stack["3D stack:
(x, y, σ)"] stack --> ext["3×3×3 local extrema
across space + scale"] ext --> thr["threshold weak responses"] thr --> kp["keypoints with (x, y, σ, dominant θ)"] ``` ### SIFT descriptor construction (slides 23–30) ```mermaid flowchart LR kp["keypoint
(x, y, σ)"] --> patch["extract patch,
rescale by 1/σ"] patch --> rot["rotate so dominant
orientation aligns with axis"] rot --> grid["4×4 spatial grid"] grid --> hist["per cell:
orientation histogram
(8 bins)"] hist --> concat["concatenate 16 × 8 = 128 numbers"] concat --> desc["SIFT descriptor"] ``` ### Why histograms of orientations beat raw patches (slide 24–25) | Property | Raw normalized patch | HoG-style descriptor | |---|---|---| | Sensitive to small misalignment | yes | no (binning absorbs it) | | Sensitive to interpolation artefacts | yes | no | | Sensitive to additive brightness | partly | no (orientations only) | | Sensitive to contrast scaling | yes | mostly invariant | | Dimensionality | high (raw pixels) | 128 | ### Feature matching pipeline (slides 32–35) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["image 1 SIFT descriptors {d_i}"] --> match["pairwise nearest-neighbour
(BFMatcher / FLANN ANN)"] B["image 2 SIFT descriptors {d_j}"] --> match match --> raw["candidate matches
(many false positives)"] raw --> ransac["RANSAC
fit a transform model
keep inliers only"] ransac --> clean["clean correspondences"] clean --> kab["Kabsch / least-squares
final transform"] ``` ### Geometric alignment decision tree (slides 36–40) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["two point clouds X, Y"] --> q1{"correspondences known?"} q1 -->|"yes"| kab["Kabsch (rigid),
or affine / homography LS"] q1 -->|"no, but roughly aligned"| icp["ICP
nearest neighbour ↔ Kabsch loop"] q1 -->|"no, totally unaligned"| ransac["RANSAC over random pairs
then refine with ICP"] ``` ### ICP loop (slide 40) ```mermaid flowchart LR init["X (source), Y (target),

content.newText:
Bigger $\sigma$ → responds to coarser-scale edges and ignores fine ones. Stacking LoGs at multiple $\sigma$s gives a **scale-space** representation, which is the basis for blob detectors (lecture 12) and SIFT.

## Lecture 12 framing: scale-normalized LoG as a blob detector

For a circular bright blob of radius $r$, the LoG at scale $\sigma$ responds most strongly when $\sigma \approx r$. To make responses comparable across scales, **scale-normalize**:

$$\sigma^2 \nabla^2 G(x, \sigma) * f(x)$$

Without the $\sigma^2$ factor, larger $\sigma$ kernels produce smaller raw responses (the Gaussian thins as it widens), so blob "wins" would be biased toward smaller scales.

Behaviour vs. blob radius $r_\text{blob}$:

- $\sigma \ll r_\text{blob}$: the Gaussian is too narrow, only sees a piece of the blob → weak response.
- $\sigma \gg r_\text{blob}$: the Gaussian is too wide, the blob is over-smoothed and contrast vanishes → weak response.
- $\sigma \approx r_\text{blob}$: strong negative minimum at the blob centre.

This is the **scale-selection** principle behind [[sift-keypoint-detection]] — find local extrema in the 3D $(x, y, \sigma)$ scale space. In practice the LoG is approximated by [[difference-of-gaussian]] for speed.

## Related

- [[laplacian-edge-detector]] — the noise-sensitive base
- [[derivative-of-gaussian]] — first-derivative analogue
- [[image-edge]]
- [[convolution-theorem]] — associativity that lets us pre-fuse the kernel
- [[difference-of-gaussian]] — fast approximation, used in SIFT
- [[sift-keypoint-detection]] — the scale-space extrema search built on this

content.newText:
---
name: Corner detection
description: Locating points where intensity changes in two independent directions, characterised by gradient distribution
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [12_feature_detectors2.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, features, corners]
---

# Corner detection

A **corner** is a local point where intensity changes in **two independent directions** — i.e. two edges meet, and the gradient direction changes abruptly across it.

## The three local-region types

For a window over the image, look at the scatter of $(I_x, I_y)$ values:

- **Flat region**: gradients cluster near the origin → no structure.
- **Edge region**: gradients form an elongated ellipse (one strong direction, one weak) → 1D structure.
- **Corner region**: gradients form an isotropic blob (both directions strong) → 2D structure.

This is the central observation: **the shape of the gradient cloud, not its mean, tells you what kind of region you're looking at**. Quantified by the [[structure-tensor|gradient covariance matrix]] and its eigenvalues — see also [[harris-corner-detector]].

## Why corners are useful

Unlike edges, corners are **localized in 2D** — they have a unique $(x, y)$ position that doesn't slide along an edge under small perturbations. This makes them the foundation of feature matching, image alignment, and structure-from-motion.

## Related

- [[image-gradient]] — the per-pixel building block
- [[structure-tensor]] — covariance of gradients in a window
- [[harris-corner-detector]] — closed-form corner score
- [[sift-keypoint-detection]] — extends to scale-invariant blobs

content.newText:
---
name: Structure tensor (gradient covariance)
description: 2×2 matrix summarising how gradients are distributed in a window — its eigenvalues classify flat / edge / corner
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [12_feature_detectors2.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, corners, linear-algebra]
---

# Structure tensor (gradient covariance)

For a window of pixels, collect their gradients $(I_x, I_y)$ and form the (optionally weighted) covariance matrix:

$$C = \sum_i w_i \begin{pmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{pmatrix}_i$$

(Or, in the lecture's notation: take the cloud of $(x_i, y_i) = (I_x, I_y)_i$, subtract the centroid, and form the weighted outer-product sum.)

The eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2$ of $C$ are the **principal moments of inertia** of the gradient cloud — they describe the lengths of the major / minor axes of the ellipse fitted to the cloud.

## Reading $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$

| Both small | Flat region |
|---|---|
| $\lambda_1 \gg \lambda_2$ | Edge (1D structure) |
| Both large, $\lambda_1 \approx \lambda_2$ | Corner (2D structure) |

This is the basis of every gradient-based corner detector. [[harris-corner-detector]] avoids computing eigenvalues explicitly via a clever combination of $\det(C)$ and $\mathrm{tr}(C)$.

## Connection to PCA

If we use uniform weights $w_i = 1$ and treat $(I_x, I_y)$ as a 2D point cloud, $C$ is exactly the PCA covariance and $\lambda_{1,2}$ are the explained variances along the principal axes:

```python
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
pca = PCA(n_components=2).fit(gradients)
mu = pca.mean_                  # centroid
eigvals = pca.explained_variance_  # λ1, λ2
eigvecs = pca.components_.T     # principal directions
```

## Related

- [[image-gradient]] — the inputs $(I_x, I_y)$
- [[corner-detection]] — the question this answers
- [[harris-corner-detector]] — eigenvalue-free response

content.newText:
---
name: Harris corner detector
description: Closed-form corner response R = det(C) − k·tr(C)² avoiding eigenvalue computation
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [12_feature_detectors2.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, corners, detector]
---

# Harris corner detector

A scalar corner response computed from the [[structure-tensor]] $C$ that uses only $\det$ and $\mathrm{tr}$ — no eigendecomposition.

$$R = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2 = \det(C) - k\,\mathrm{tr}(C)^2, \qquad k \approx 0.05$$

## How $R$ classifies a region

| Region | $\lambda_1$, $\lambda_2$ | $\det = \lambda_1\lambda_2$ | $\mathrm{tr} = \lambda_1+\lambda_2$ | $R$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flat | both small | tiny | small | small (near 0) |
| Edge | one large, one small | small | large | **large negative** |
| Corner | both large | large | large | **large positive** |

So: pick pixels where $R$ is large and positive.

## Pipeline

1. Compute $I_x$, $I_y$ (Sobel).
2. Form per-pixel $I_x^2$, $I_y^2$, $I_x I_y$.
3. Smooth each with a Gaussian window — this aggregates over a window without explicit summation.
4. Compute $R$ at each pixel.
5. Threshold + [[non-maximum-suppression]] (e.g. local-max within a small window).

```python
from skimage.feature import corner_harris, corner_peaks
coords = corner_peaks(corner_harris(image_gray),
                      min_distance=5, threshold_rel=0.02)
```

## Properties

**Advantages**

- **Rotation-invariant** — eigenvalues of a covariance matrix don't change under rotation.
- **Partially intensity-invariant** — uses gradients (differences), so additive shifts cancel.
- Cheap to compute.

**Limitations**

- **Not scale-invariant** — doubling the image changes which windows look "corner-like".
- Sensitive to noise.
- Manual tuning of window size and threshold.

For scale invariance, see [[sift-keypoint-detection]].

## Related

- [[corner-detection]] — the problem
- [[structure-tensor]] — what $C$ is
- [[non-maximum-suppression]] — used for peak picking
- [[sift-keypoint-detection]] — addresses Harris's scale limitation

```python
from skimage.feature import corner_harris, corner_peaks
coords = corner_peaks(corner_harris(image_gray),
                      min_distance=5, threshold_rel=0.02)
```

## Properties

**Advantages**

- **Rotation-invariant** — eigenvalues of a covariance matrix don't change under rotation.
- **Partially intensity-invariant** — uses gradients (differences), so additive shifts cancel.
- Cheap to compute.

**Limitations**

- **Not scale-invariant** — doubling the image changes which windows look "corner-like".
- Sensitive to noise.
- Manual tuning of window size and threshold.

For scale invariance, see [[sift-keypoint-detection]].

## Related

- [[corner-detection]] — the problem
- [[structure-tensor]] — what $C$ is
- [[non-maximum-suppression]] — used for peak picking
- [[sift-keypoint-detection]] — addresses Harris's scale limitation

---
name: Difference of Gaussian (DoG)
description: Cheap approximation to scale-normalised LoG by subtracting two Gaussian-blurred copies at adjacent scales
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [12_feature_detectors2.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, scale-space, blob-detection]
---

# Difference of Gaussian (DoG)

For SIFT-style scale-space blob detection, we want the [[laplacian-of-gaussian|scale-normalised LoG]] response at many $\sigma$ values. Computing each LoG directly is expensive. The **DoG approximation** uses subtractions of pre-computed Gaussian-smoothed images:

$$G(x, s\sigma) - G(x, \sigma) \approx (s - 1)\,\sigma^2 \nabla^2 G(x, \sigma)$$

So the difference of two Gaussian-blurred images at adjacent scales is, up to a constant, the scale-normalised LoG.

## Why this is fast

A Gaussian pyramid is built by blurring once per scale level. Each DoG image is a single subtraction between two adjacent levels — essentially free compared to convolving with a separate LoG kernel at each scale.

## Pipeline (slide 19)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["source image"] --> g0["G(σ)"]
    src --> g1["G(kσ)"]
    src --> g2["G(k²σ)"]
    src --> g3["G(k³σ)"]
    g1 --> d1["DoG = G(kσ) − G(σ)"]
    g0 --> d1
    g2 --> d2["DoG = G(k²σ) − G(kσ)"]
    g1 --> d2
    g3 --> d3["DoG = G(k³σ) − G(k²σ)"]
    g2 --> d3
    d1 & d2 & d3 --> stack["3D scale-space stack"]
```

## How the approximation falls out

Heat-equation identity: $\partial G / \partial \sigma = \sigma \nabla^2 G$. A finite difference with step $\Delta\sigma = (s-1)\sigma$ gives the displayed equation. Smaller $s$ → tighter approximation, but larger $s$ → wider scale coverage with fewer pyramid levels. SIFT uses $s = 2^{1/3}$ so that three DoGs cover an octave.

## Related

- [[laplacian-of-gaussian]] — what this approximates
- [[sift-keypoint-detection]] — uses DoG as its scale-space operator
- [[derivative-of-gaussian]] — first-order analogue

content.newText:
---
name: SIFT keypoint detection
description: Scale-invariant feature detection via local extrema in a 3D Gaussian/DoG scale-space
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [12_feature_detectors2.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, sift, blob-detection, scale-space]
---

# SIFT keypoint detection

The detector half of SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform). Goal: find **points of interest** — small "blobs" with rich local structure that have a well-defined position **and** a characteristic scale and orientation.

## Why blobs, not corners

[[harris-corner-detector|Harris]] finds corners but isn't scale-invariant. SIFT instead finds **circular blobs** at the scale where they are most distinctive, using the [[laplacian-of-gaussian|scale-normalised LoG]] (in practice approximated by [[difference-of-gaussian|DoG]]).

Recall: the scale-normalised LoG $\sigma^2 \nabla^2 G * f$ is maximally negative at the centre of a bright blob when $\sigma \approx r_\text{blob}$. So the keypoint's $\sigma$ tells you the blob's size.

## Pipeline (slides 16–21)

1. **Gaussian pyramid**: compute $G(\sigma_0) * I,\, G(k\sigma_0) * I,\, G(k^2\sigma_0) * I, \dots$ across multiple octaves.
2. **DoG pyramid**: subtract adjacent Gaussian levels.
3. **3D extremum search**: for each pixel in each DoG level, check whether it is greater than (or less than) all 26 neighbours in a $3\times 3\times 3$ window across $(x, y, \sigma)$. Each extremum is a candidate keypoint with a location *and* a scale.
4. **Threshold** weak responses (low contrast) and reject keypoints on edges (where one Hessian eigenvalue dominates — same idea as Harris's edge rejection).
5. **Dominant orientation**: compute a histogram of gradient orientations in a window around each keypoint; the peak is the keypoint's orientation. (See [[sift-descriptor]].)

Output: keypoints carrying $(x, y, \sigma, \theta)$.

## Why detect across scale

Same physical feature appears at different sizes when the camera zooms or moves. By detecting at the scale where the LoG response peaks, the same blob lights up regardless of zoom — that's **scale invariance**.

## Why orientation-normalize

Two views of the same scene are rotated relative to each other. Recording each blob's dominant gradient orientation lets us rotate descriptors into a canonical frame before comparing.

## Related

- [[laplacian-of-gaussian]], [[difference-of-gaussian]] — the underlying scale-space operators
- [[sift-descriptor]] — what gets computed at each keypoint
- [[non-maximum-suppression]] — used in extremum filtering
- [[harris-corner-detector]] — what SIFT improves on (scale invariance)

content.newText:
---
name: SIFT descriptor
description: 128-D feature vector built from a 4×4 grid of 8-bin gradient-orientation histograms, scale- and rotation-normalised
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [12_feature_detectors2.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, sift, descriptor]
---

# SIFT descriptor

Once a keypoint $(x, y, \sigma, \theta)$ has been found by [[sift-keypoint-detection]], we need a **descriptor** — a vector that summarises the local appearance and that we can compare to descriptors from other images.

## Why not just compare normalized pixel patches?

Even after rescaling by $1/\sigma$ and rotating to align the dominant orientation, raw pixel patches are:

- Sensitive to small misalignments (a 1-pixel shift changes everything).
- Sensitive to interpolation artefacts from rescaling/rotating.
- Sensitive to noise.
- High-dimensional (one dimension per pixel).
- Sensitive to illumination changes.

Histograms of gradient orientations are much better:

- Gradient orientations are invariant to additive brightness shifts and largely robust to contrast scaling.
- Histogramming absorbs small spatial misalignments — bins are stable.
- Compact: 128 numbers vs. thousands of pixels.

## Construction (slides 25–29)

1. Take the patch around the keypoint, rescaled by $1/\sigma$ and rotated so the dominant orientation aligns with a reference axis.
2. Divide into a **$4\times 4$ spatial grid** (16 cells).
3. In each cell, compute the histogram of gradient orientations into **8 angular bins**.
4. Reorder bins so the dominant orientation sits at index 0 (rotation normalization).
5. Concatenate all 16 histograms → **$16 \times 8 = 128$-D descriptor**.

## Comparing descriptors

Two common metrics (slide 31):

- **L2 distance**: $d(H_1, H_2) = \sqrt{\sum_k (H_1(k) - H_2(k))^2}$ (smaller is better).
- **Normalized cross-correlation**: $\frac{\sum_k (H_1 - \bar{H}_1)(H_2 - \bar{H}_2)}{\sqrt{\sum_k(H_1-\bar{H}_1)^2}\sqrt{\sum_k(H_2-\bar{H}_2)^2}}$ (closer to 1 is better).

## Where this appears

SIFT descriptors are the input to image **stitching** (panoramas), **alignment**, **object recognition**, and pre-deep-learning **retrieval**. Modern learned descriptors (SuperPoint, etc.) replace the histogram step but keep the same overall pipeline.

## Related

- [[sift-keypoint-detection]] — produces the keypoints we describe
- [[image-gradient]] — the input to the histograms
- [[feature-matching-ransac]] — what we do with descriptors next

content.newText:
---
name: Feature matching and RANSAC
description: Cross-image descriptor matching plus RANSAC to filter out false-positive correspondences
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [12_feature_detectors2.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, matching, robust-estimation]
---

# Feature matching + RANSAC

## Matching

Given two sets of [[sift-descriptor|SIFT descriptors]] — one per image — find correspondences:

- **Brute force** (`cv2.BFMatcher`): for each descriptor in image 1, find its nearest neighbour in image 2 by L2 or normalized correlation. $O(n_1 \cdot n_2)$ but exact.
- **Approximate nearest neighbour** (FLANN, `cv2.FlannBasedMatcher`): KD-tree / hashing structures bring it down to roughly $O(n \log n)$ at the cost of occasional missed matches.

Common refinement: **Lowe's ratio test** — keep a match only if the nearest neighbour is significantly closer than the second nearest (e.g. ratio < 0.7). Filters out ambiguous matches.

## Why we need RANSAC

Even after the ratio test, a substantial fraction of matches are wrong (similar local patches in unrelated places). A naive least-squares fit on all matches will be dragged off by outliers.

## RANSAC (random sample consensus)

```text
best_inliers = []
for _ in range(N):
    sample = random subset of matches (minimum needed to fit model)
    model = fit_transform(sample)
    inliers = matches whose error under model < ε
    if |inliers| > |best_inliers|:
        best_inliers = inliers
return refit_transform(best_inliers)
```

Key knobs:

- $N$: number of trials. Choose so the probability of *some* trial having an all-inlier sample is high (e.g. 99%) given an estimated outlier rate.
- $\varepsilon$: inlier-error threshold (in pixels for image alignment).
- Minimum sample size: 2 for a line, 3 for an affine, 4 for a homography, etc.

## Output

A subset of correspondences (the inliers) plus a fitted geometric transform between the two images.

## Related

- [[sift-descriptor]] — what we're matching
- [[kabsch-algorithm]] — used to refit the transform on inliers (rigid case)
- [[icp-algorithm]] — alternative when correspondences are unknown
- [[non-maximum-suppression]] — different style of robust filtering

content.newText:
---
name: Kabsch algorithm
description: Closed-form least-squares rigid-body alignment of two point clouds with known correspondences via SVD
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [12_feature_detectors2.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, alignment, svd]
---

# Kabsch algorithm

Closed-form solution to the **rigid-body alignment** problem: given two corresponding point sets $\{x_i\}, \{y_i\}$ in $\mathbb{R}^N$, find rotation $R$ and translation $t$ that minimize:

$$R^*, t^* = \arg\min_{R, t} \sum_i \|R x_i + t - y_i\|^2$$

## Steps

1. **Subtract centroids**:
   $$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i x_i, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i y_i, \quad \tilde{x}_i = x_i - \bar{x}, \quad \tilde{y}_i = y_i - \bar{y}$$
2. **Cross-covariance matrix**: $H = \sum_i \tilde{x}_i \tilde{y}_i^\top$.
3. **SVD**: $H = U\Sigma V^\top$.
4. **Optimal rotation**: $R = V U^\top$.
5. **Translation**: $t = \bar{y} - R\bar{x}$.

(In edge cases where $\det(VU^\top) = -1$, flip the sign of the last column of $V$ before computing $R$ to avoid a reflection.)

## When to use

- You have **known correspondences** between two clouds (e.g. from [[feature-matching-ransac]] inliers).
- You want a **rigid** transform (rotation + translation, no scaling, no shearing). For affine or projective fits, swap step 4 for the corresponding least-squares closed form (or use homogeneous coordinates).

## When not to use

- Correspondences are unknown → use [[icp-algorithm]] (which calls Kabsch in its inner loop).
- Outliers dominate → wrap Kabsch in [[feature-matching-ransac|RANSAC]].

## Related

- [[icp-algorithm]] — uses Kabsch each iteration on tentative correspondences
- [[feature-matching-ransac]] — produces the correspondences Kabsch needs

content.newText:
---
name: Iterative Closest Points (ICP)
description: Iterative point-cloud alignment when correspondences are unknown — alternates nearest-neighbour matching with Kabsch
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [12_feature_detectors2.pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, alignment, iterative]
---

# Iterative Closest Points (ICP)

Aligns two point clouds when **correspondences are unknown**. Assumes the clouds are already roughly aligned; ICP refines that alignment.

## Algorithm

```python
while not converged:
    indices = nearest_neighbor(X, Y)         # tentative correspondences
    R, t = kabsch(X, Y[indices])             # fit rigid transform
    X = R @ X + t                            # apply
```

Each iteration:

1. For each point in source $X$, find its **nearest neighbour** in target $Y$. These are *guesses* at the correspondences.
2. Run [[kabsch-algorithm]] on the corresponded pairs.
3. Apply the transform and repeat.

Convergence test: change in transform (or sum of squared distances) below a threshold.

## Why it needs a good initialization

ICP minimises a non-convex objective. With a poor initial alignment, the nearest-neighbour step pairs the wrong points, Kabsch fits a wrong transform, and ICP locks into a bad local minimum.

## When correspondences are unknown AND the clouds aren't roughly aligned

Two options (slide 39):

- **RANSAC over random pairs** — sample minimal point sets, fit candidate transforms, count inliers. Expensive and inaccurate alone, so usually followed by ICP for refinement.
- **Brute-force search over rotations and translations** — also expensive and inaccurate.

The standard recipe is RANSAC for global, ICP for local refinement.

## Variants

- **Point-to-plane ICP**: error metric is distance from source point to local target plane → faster convergence.
- **Robust ICP**: down-weights pairs with large residuals (Huber/Tukey loss).
- **Trimmed ICP**: drops the worst-fitting fraction of pairs each iteration → outlier-tolerant.

## Related

- [[kabsch-algorithm]] — the inner step
- [[feature-matching-ransac]] — global initialization step

source: 12_feature_detectors2.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---

# Lecture 12 — Feature detectors 2 (flashcards)

Q:: How do you tell flat / edge / corner regions apart from the *distribution* of gradients in a window?
A:: Flat = compact blob near origin (both eigenvalues small). Edge = elongated ellipse (one eigenvalue large, the other small). Corner = isotropic blob (both eigenvalues large and similar).

Q:: Why are the eigenvalues of the gradient covariance matrix the right tool to classify a window?
A:: They are the principal moments of inertia of the gradient cloud — the lengths of the major and minor axes of its ellipse. The cloud's *shape*, not its mean, encodes whether structure is 0D, 1D, or 2D.

Q:: Write the Harris corner response and explain why it works.
A:: $R = \det(C) - k\,\mathrm{tr}(C)^2 = \lambda_1\lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)^2$ with $k\approx 0.05$. Large positive when both eigenvalues are large (corner), large negative when one dominates (edge), near zero when both are small (flat).

Q:: Why does Harris use $\det/\mathrm{tr}$ instead of computing eigenvalues directly?
A:: Both are computable in closed form from the four entries of a 2×2 matrix; eigendecomposition is more expensive and unnecessary for a scalar score.

Q:: Two key invariances Harris has, and one it lacks?
A:: Has: rotation invariance (eigenvalues of a covariance don't change under rotation) and partial intensity invariance (uses gradients, not raw values). Lacks: scale invariance.

Q:: What does it mean for SIFT to be "scale-invariant" and how does it achieve it?
A:: The same physical blob produces the same descriptor regardless of zoom. Achieved by detecting keypoints as 3D extrema in $(x, y, \sigma)$ scale-space — each keypoint carries the $\sigma$ at which it is most distinctive, so the descriptor is computed in that intrinsic scale.

Q:: Why is the Laplacian of Gaussian *scale-normalised* with a $\sigma^2$ factor?
A:: Without normalization, the LoG response shrinks as $\sigma$ grows (the Gaussian thins as it widens). The $\sigma^2$ factor cancels this, making responses comparable across scales so we can pick the $\sigma$ at which a blob's response peaks.

Q:: At what relationship between $\sigma$ and blob radius $r$ does the LoG respond most strongly?
A:: $\sigma \approx r$. Smaller $\sigma$ "doesn't see" the whole blob; larger $\sigma$ over-smooths it and contrast vanishes.

Q:: Why use Difference of Gaussian instead of LoG in practice?
A:: $G(x, s\sigma) - G(x, \sigma) \approx (s-1)\sigma^2 \nabla^2 G$, so DoG approximates the scale-normalised LoG with one *subtraction* between Gaussian-pyramid levels — far cheaper than computing each LoG.

Q:: How are SIFT keypoints extracted from a DoG pyramid?
A:: 3×3×3 local extrema in $(x, y, \sigma)$ — each candidate is greater (or less) than all 26 neighbours across space and scale. Followed by thresholding on contrast and rejection of edge-like points.

Q:: Why doesn't SIFT use raw, scale-and-rotation-normalised pixel patches as descriptors?
A:: Patches are sensitive to small misalignments, interpolation artefacts, noise, illumination changes, and are high-dimensional. Histograms of gradient orientations are robust to all of these.

Q:: Why are gradient orientations robust to additive brightness changes and contrast scaling?
A:: Adding a constant to every pixel cancels in the difference $I(x+1) - I(x-1)$. Multiplying by a constant scales gradients uniformly so their *orientations* (angles) are unchanged.

Q:: Describe the SIFT descriptor's structure and dimensionality.
A:: 4×4 spatial grid over the keypoint patch, 8-bin orientation histogram per cell, concatenated → 4 × 4 × 8 = 128 numbers.

Q:: Why is the SIFT descriptor's bin order "rotated" so the dominant orientation is at index 0?
A:: To make the descriptor invariant to image rotation. Two rotated views of the same blob then produce the same vector (up to local appearance differences).

Q:: Why use FLANN-style approximate nearest neighbours instead of brute force when matching SIFT features?
A:: With $n_1, n_2$ on the order of thousands, $O(n_1 n_2)$ is too slow. KD-tree / hashing structures give roughly $O(n \log n)$ matching at the cost of occasionally missing the true nearest neighbour.

Q:: What is Lowe's ratio test and what does it filter?
A:: For each query descriptor, accept the nearest match only if its distance is less than (e.g.) 0.7 times the second-nearest distance. Filters ambiguous matches where two candidates in the second image look almost equally similar.

Q:: Why does naive least-squares fail on raw matched-feature pairs, and what does RANSAC do?
A:: A few wildly wrong correspondences pull the least-squares fit far from the truth. RANSAC repeatedly samples a minimal subset, fits a transform, counts inliers under that transform, and keeps the largest consensus set.

Q:: What is the Kabsch algorithm computing, and why is SVD involved?
A:: Closed-form least-squares rigid alignment of corresponded point clouds. After centring, the rotation that aligns them is $R = V U^\top$ where $H = U\Sigma V^\top$ is the SVD of the cross-covariance $H = \sum_i \tilde{x}_i \tilde{y}_i^\top$. SVD is the standard way to find the orthogonal matrix nearest to $H$.

Q:: Explain what each iteration of ICP does and why it converges only when the clouds start "roughly aligned".
A:: Each iteration: (1) match each source point to its nearest neighbour in the target; (2) run Kabsch on those pairs; (3) apply. Bad initialization → wrong nearest neighbours → wrong transform → ICP locks into a local minimum far from the true alignment.

Q:: When correspondences are completely unknown and the clouds are unaligned, what's the standard recipe?
A:: Use RANSAC over random point pairs to estimate a coarse rigid transform, then run ICP from that initialization to refine.

Q:: Why does the Harris detector "rely on manually set window sizes and thresholds" matter for downstream work?
A:: It hardcodes a scale and a sensitivity. Cross-image matching breaks if the chosen window doesn't match the relevant feature size — this is exactly the limitation SIFT's scale-space machinery removes.

Q:: One sentence: when do you reach for SIFT vs. Harris vs. Canny?
A:: Canny when you want a binary edge map; Harris when you want quick rotation-invariant corners at one scale; SIFT when you need to *match* features across images of varying scale and orientation.

--- tags: [lecture] course: Computer-Vision source: 13_binary_object_characterization(1).pdf lecture: 13 created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 13 — Binary object characterization ## Overview After we have a **binary mask** of the image (foreground / background), what can we measure about each object? Two big topics: 1. **Connected components** — split the binary image into individual objects. Two algorithm families: region-growing (BFS/DFS from seeds) and two-pass sequence labelling with a union-find equivalence table. 2. **Geometric and statistical properties** — area, perimeter, convex hull, compactness, solidity, centre of mass (first moment), orientation (second moment / eigenvector). These are the building blocks for classical shape-based recognition before deep learning. ## Key concepts - [[binary-image]] — what a binary mask is and how it's obtained - [[connected-components]] — labelling, 4- vs 8-connectivity, two algorithms - [[shape-descriptors]] — area, perimeter, convex hull, compactness, solidity - [[image-moments]] — centre of mass, orientation via eigenvector of $M$, eccentricity ## Equations Centre of mass / first moment (slide 13): $$\mu = (\bar{x}, \bar{y}) = \frac{1}{N}\!\left(\sum_i x_i,\; \sum_i y_i\right)$$ Central second moments (slide 15): $$\mu_{20} = \sum_{i=1}^N (x_i - \bar{x})^2, \quad \mu_{02} = \sum_{i=1}^N (y_i - \bar{y})^2, \quad \mu_{11} = \sum_{i=1}^N (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$$ Second-moment matrix and orientation: $$M = \begin{pmatrix} \mu_{20} & \mu_{11} \\ \mu_{11} & \mu_{02} \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{atan2}(2\mu_{11},\, \mu_{20} - \mu_{02})$$ Note: in slide 15 the lecture wrote $\mu_{20}$ and $\mu_{02}$ without the squares; the conventional definition (and the one consistent with the matrix and $\theta$ formula) requires the squared central deviations as written above. Major / minor axis lengths and eccentricity: $$L_\text{major} \propto \sqrt{\lambda_1}, \quad L_\text{minor} \propto \sqrt{\lambda_2}, \quad e = \sqrt{1 - \lambda_2/\lambda_1}$$ where $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2$ are the eigenvalues of $M$. Compactness / circularity (slide 12): $$f_\text{circ} = \frac{4\pi\,\text{Area}}{\text{Perimeter}^2}$$ (equals 1 for a perfect circle, less for everything else) Solidity: $$\text{solidity} = \frac{\text{Area}}{\text{Area of convex hull}}$$ ## Diagrams ### From image to per-object descriptors ```mermaid flowchart LR img["grayscale image"] -->|"threshold (Otsu, DL mask)"| bin["binary image"] bin -->|"connected components
(4- or 8-connectivity)"| labels["labelled regions"] labels --> per["per-object descriptors"] per --> A["area, perimeter"] per --> H["convex hull, solidity"] per --> C["compactness"] per --> M["centroid (1st moment)"] per --> O["orientation, eccentricity (2nd moment)"] ``` ### 4- vs 8-connectivity (slide 5) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph C4["4-connectivity"] n1["N"] --- p1((P)) s1["S"] --- p1 e1["E"] --- p1 w1["W"] --- p1 end subgraph C8["8-connectivity"] nw["NW"] --- p2((P)) n2["N"] --- p2 ne["NE"] --- p2 w2["W"] --- p2 e2["E"] --- p2 sw["SW"] --- p2 s2["S"] --- p2 se["SE"] --- p2 end ``` Tradeoff: 4-connectivity over-segments diagonal touches; 8-connectivity merges objects that only touch at a corner. Neither is universally right. ### Region-growing connected components (slide 4) ```mermaid flowchart TD start["unlabelled foreground pixel?"] -->|"yes"| seed["assign new label"] seed --> bfs["BFS/DFS to all connected foreground neighbours,
stamp them with the same label"] bfs --> done{"any unlabelled
foreground left?"} done -->|"yes"| start done -->|"no"| out["per-pixel label image"] ``` ### Two-pass sequence labelling (slides 6–8) ```mermaid flowchart TD s1["pass 1: raster scan,
assign label using
already-seen neighbours"] --> case{"neighbour pattern?"} case -->|"all 0"| nl["new label"] case -->|"one labelled neighbour"| inh["inherit it"] case -->|"two labelled neighbours,
same label"| inh case -->|"two labelled neighbours,
different labels"| eq["pick one,
record equivalence in union-find"] nl --> p1done inh --> p1done eq --> p1done["pass 1 finished"] p1done --> resolve["resolve equivalence classes
with union-find"] resolve --> p2["pass 2: relabel each pixel
to the canonical class label"] ``` ### Reading the second-moment matrix (slides 13–15) ```mermaid flowchart LR pix["foreground pixels of one object"] --> centre["centroid (1st moment)
μ = mean of (x_i, y_i)"] pix --> M["M = central 2nd moments
μ20, μ02, μ11"] M --> eig["eigenvectors / eigenvalues"] eig --> orient["orientation θ
= angle of largest eigenvector"] eig --> axes["axis lengths
L ∝ √λ"] eig --> ecc["eccentricity
e = √(1 − λ2/λ1)"] ``` This is structurally identical to the [[structure-tensor]] from lecture 12 — same eigendecomposition, but applied to **pixel coordinates** here vs. **gradient values** there. | Comparison | [[structure-tensor]] (lec 12) | Image moments (lec 13) | |---|---|---| | Operates on | gradient values $(I_x, I_y)$ in a window | foreground pixel coordinates $(x, y)$ | | Eigen-axes describe | gradient distribution → corner / edge / flat | object shape → aspect ratio, orientation | ## Open questions - The lecture wrote $\mu_{20}$, $\mu_{02}$ without the squared deviations. Conventionally these *are* the squared central moments — is the slide using a slightly different normalization, or is it a typo? - For thin objects (e.g. wires), 8-connectivity is essential or they break apart. For touching circles (coins) it merges them. Is there a standard scheme that adapts per-object? - Solidity is widely used to filter out concave / fragmented detections. What's a good threshold for "clean" coin detection from the slide-10 example? ## Connections to other lectures - The binary-image step links to [[non-maximum-suppression]] (filtering candidate detections) and to morphological operations briefly mentioned (slide 10). - The second-moment story is identical machinery to [[structure-tensor]] / [[harris-corner-detector]] from lecture 12 — same PCA / eigendecomposition, different inputs. - Modern pipelines often produce the binary mask via deep-learning segmentation (lecture 18) rather than thresholding.

content.newText:
---
name: Binary image
description: Image with only foreground/background pixels, obtained by thresholding intensities or a predicted mask
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [13_binary_object_characterization(1).pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, segmentation, binary]
---

# Binary image

A **binary image** has only two pixel values — typically 0 (background) and 1 (foreground). It's the input format for most classical shape-analysis tools.

## Two ways to get one

1. **Thresholding intensity values**: pick a cutoff and binarize. The classic data-driven choice is **Otsu's method**, which minimizes the within-class variance of foreground / background.
2. **Thresholding a predicted mask**: in modern pipelines, a deep-learning segmentation model outputs a per-pixel probability or class map, which is thresholded into a binary mask.

## Why binary

Once you have a binary mask, all the geometric / topological tools — [[connected-components]], [[shape-descriptors]], [[image-moments]] — are well-defined and cheap to compute. They were the foundation of pre-deep-learning industrial vision: counting objects, sorting parts, OCR pre-processing, blob analysis.

## Typical pre-processing

Raw thresholding usually produces noisy masks: small spurious blobs, holes inside objects, jagged boundaries. Standard fixes (mentioned slide 10):

- **Morphological opening** (erode then dilate) → removes small foreground specks.
- **Morphological closing** (dilate then erode) → fills small holes.
- Connected-component filtering by area: drop labels with too few pixels.

## Related

- [[connected-components]] — the next step
- [[shape-descriptors]], [[image-moments]] — what you measure on each object

content.newText:
---
name: Connected components
description: Identifying isolated objects in a binary image as maximal sets of connected foreground pixels
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [13_binary_object_characterization(1).pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, binary, graph-algorithm]
---

# Connected components

Given a [[binary-image]], a **connected component** is a maximal set of foreground pixels in which every two pixels are joined by a path of foreground pixels. Each component is one isolated object.

## Connectivity choice

A pixel's neighbours can be defined two ways:

- **4-connectivity**: N, S, E, W (axis-aligned).
- **8-connectivity**: also includes the four diagonal neighbours.

Tradeoffs:

| Choice | Tends to over- | Tends to under- |
|---|---|---|
| 4-connectivity | split objects that touch only diagonally | — |
| 8-connectivity | — | merge objects whose corners touch |

Neither is universally right; the right choice depends on the scene (thin diagonal wires need 8; touching coins want 4).

## Algorithms

### Region growing (BFS / DFS)

Pseudocode:

```text
1. Pick an unlabelled foreground pixel p; assign it a new label.
2. Recursively (BFS or DFS) propagate that label to every connected foreground neighbour.
3. When the queue empties, repeat from step 1 until every foreground pixel is labelled.
```

Simple but two passes through memory per object.

### Two-pass sequence labelling (Union-Find)

Pseudocode:

```text
Pass 1 (raster scan top-left → bottom-right):
  For each foreground pixel p, look at already-scanned neighbours
  (B = above, C = left, D = above-left for 8-connectivity):
    no labelled neighbour       → assign new label
    one labelled neighbour      → inherit
    two labelled neighbours
      same label                → inherit
      different labels          → inherit either, record union-find merge
Resolve equivalence classes: union-find collapses each class to a canonical label.
Pass 2: replace every label by its canonical class label.
```

Faster in practice (single-pass cache-friendly access) — what `cv2.connectedComponentsWithStats` and friends are doing under the hood.

## Reference calls

```python
# OpenCV
num_labels, labels, stats, centroids = cv2.connectedComponentsWithStats(
    binary_img, connectivity=8)
areas = stats[1:, cv2.CC_STAT_AREA]   # exclude background label 0

# scikit-image
from skimage.measure import label, regionprops_table
labels_sk = label(binary_img, connectivity=2)   # 1 → 4-C, 2 → 8-C
props = regionprops_table(labels_sk, properties=("label", "area"))
```

## Related

- [[binary-image]] — the input
- [[shape-descriptors]], [[image-moments]] — per-component measurements

# Connected components

Given a [[binary-image]], a **connected component** is a maximal set of foreground pixels in which every two pixels are joined by a path of foreground pixels. Each component is one isolated object.

## Connectivity choice

A pixel's neighbours can be defined two ways:

- **4-connectivity**: N, S, E, W (axis-aligned).
- **8-connectivity**: also includes the four diagonal neighbours.

Tradeoffs:

| Choice | Tends to over- | Tends to under- |
|---|---|---|
| 4-connectivity | split objects that touch only diagonally | — |
| 8-connectivity | — | merge objects whose corners touch |

Neither is universally right; the right choice depends on the scene (thin diagonal wires need 8; touching coins want 4).

## Algorithms

### Region growing (BFS / DFS)

Pseudocode:

```text
1. Pick an unlabelled foreground pixel p; assign it a new label.
2. Recursively (BFS or DFS) propagate that label to every connected foreground neighbour.
3. When the queue empties, repeat from step 1 until every foreground pixel is labelled.
```

Simple but two passes through memory per object.

### Two-pass sequence labelling (Union-Find)

Pseudocode:

```text
Pass 1 (raster scan top-left → bottom-right):
  For each foreground pixel p, look at already-scanned neighbours
  (B = above, C = left, D = above-left for 8-connectivity):
    no labelled neighbour       → assign new label
    one labelled neighbour      → inherit
    two labelled neighbours
      same label                → inherit
      different labels          → inherit either, record union-find merge
Resolve equivalence classes: union-find collapses each class to a canonical label.
Pass 2: replace every label by its canonical class label.
```

Faster in practice (single-pass cache-friendly access) — what `cv2.connectedComponentsWithStats` and friends are doing under the hood.

## Reference calls

```python
# OpenCV
num_labels, labels, stats, centroids = cv2.connectedComponentsWithStats(
    binary_img, connectivity=8)
areas = stats[1:, cv2.CC_STAT_AREA]   # exclude background label 0

# scikit-image
from skimage.measure import label, regionprops_table
labels_sk = label(binary_img, connectivity=2)   # 1 → 4-C, 2 → 8-C
props = regionprops_table(labels_sk, properties=("label", "area"))
```

- [[shape-descriptors]], [[image-moments]] — per-component measurements

---
name: Shape descriptors (area, perimeter, hull, compactness, solidity)
description: Geometric per-object measurements derived from a binary mask
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [13_binary_object_characterization(1).pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, binary, geometry]
---

# Shape descriptors

Per-object geometric measurements computed on a [[connected-components|connected component]]. Once you have a binary mask, these are cheap, interpretable, and surprisingly useful — they were the workhorses of pre-deep-learning industrial vision and still appear as features in classical pipelines.

## Area

Number of foreground pixels in the component.

## Perimeter

Two common ways:

- **Grid-count approximation**: a foreground pixel is a *boundary* pixel if it has at least one 4-neighbour that is 0. For each foreground pixel, add 1 to the perimeter for each missing 4-neighbour. Fast but biased on rotated shapes (overestimates).
- **Marching squares**: trace the contour as a polyline and sum segment lengths. More accurate.

## Convex hull

The smallest convex polygon containing every foreground pixel. Available via `cv2.convexHull` / `skimage.morphology.convex_hull_image`. Useful as a reference shape for **solidity**.

## Compactness / circularity

$$f_\text{circ} = \frac{4\pi\,\text{Area}}{\text{Perimeter}^2}$$

- Equal to **1** for a perfect circle (any reasonable perimeter approximation aside).
- Falls toward 0 as the shape gets less round (more elongated, more boundary irregularity).

## Solidity

$$\text{solidity} = \frac{\text{Area}}{\text{Area of convex hull}}$$

- Equal to 1 for any convex shape (rectangles, circles, triangles).
- Falls below 1 for objects with concavities or holes (a horse silhouette has many concavities under the belly / between legs).

## When each metric is informative

- **Compactness** discriminates round from elongated objects.
- **Solidity** discriminates blocky / convex from articulated / concave objects.
- **Area + bounding-box dimensions** quickly filter spurious tiny detections.

## Related

- [[binary-image]], [[connected-components]] — the prerequisites
- [[image-moments]] — orientation / eccentricity, complementary descriptors

content.newText:
---
name: Image moments (centroid, orientation, eccentricity)
description: First and second moments of a binary object — centre of mass and second-moment matrix giving orientation and aspect
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [13_binary_object_characterization(1).pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, binary, statistics, eigendecomposition]
---

# Image moments

Statistical descriptors of a [[connected-components|connected component]] that capture position, orientation, and aspect ratio.

## First moment — centre of mass

$$\mu = (\bar{x}, \bar{y}) = \frac{1}{N}\!\left(\sum_i x_i,\; \sum_i y_i\right)$$

Where $\{(x_i, y_i)\}$ are the foreground pixel coordinates and $N$ is the area.

## Second-moment matrix

Central second moments:

$$\mu_{20} = \sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2, \quad \mu_{02} = \sum_i (y_i - \bar{y})^2, \quad \mu_{11} = \sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$$

Form the matrix:

$$M = \begin{pmatrix} \mu_{20} & \mu_{11} \\ \mu_{11} & \mu_{02} \end{pmatrix}$$

This is the (unnormalized) **covariance matrix of pixel coordinates**.

## Reading $M$

The eigendecomposition of $M$ gives:

- **Orientation** $\theta$ — angle of the largest eigenvector. Closed-form:
  $$\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{atan2}(2\mu_{11},\, \mu_{20} - \mu_{02})$$
  This is the **axis of least second moment** — rotating the object so $\theta = 0$ minimizes $\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2$ along the new axis (intuitively, "lay the object on its side").
- **Major / minor axis lengths**: $L_\text{major} \propto \sqrt{\lambda_1}$, $L_\text{minor} \propto \sqrt{\lambda_2}$ with $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2$.
- **Eccentricity**:
  $$e = \sqrt{1 - \frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}}$$
  Equal to 0 for a circle ($\lambda_1 = \lambda_2$), approaching 1 for very elongated shapes.

## Same machinery as the structure tensor

Image moments and the [[structure-tensor]] use the same linear-algebra recipe — covariance matrix → eigendecomposition → orientation + axes. The only difference is what is being summarized:

| Object | Inputs |
|---|---|
| Image moments (this note) | foreground pixel coordinates $(x, y)$ |
| Structure tensor | gradient values $(I_x, I_y)$ in a window |

## Related

- [[binary-image]], [[connected-components]] — the prerequisites
- [[shape-descriptors]] — complementary metrics (area, perimeter, hull, compactness)
- [[structure-tensor]] — same algebra applied to gradients
- [[harris-corner-detector]] — uses the same eigenvalue idea

---

# Lecture 13 — Binary object characterization (flashcards)

Q:: Name two common ways to obtain a binary mask from a grayscale image.
A:: Thresholding intensity values (e.g. Otsu's method picks the threshold automatically), and thresholding a deep-learning model's per-pixel output map.

Q:: Define a connected component in a binary image.
A:: A maximal set of foreground pixels in which any two pixels are joined by a path of foreground neighbours.

Q:: When does 4-connectivity over-segment, and when does 8-connectivity over-merge?
A:: 4-C splits objects whose only contact is diagonal (the diagonal neighbours aren't counted as adjacent). 8-C merges objects whose corners barely touch.

Q:: Outline the region-growing connected-components algorithm.
A:: Pick an unlabelled foreground pixel, give it a new label, BFS/DFS-flood-fill the same label to every connected foreground neighbour, repeat until no unlabelled foreground remains.

Q:: In two-pass sequence labelling, what happens when a pixel sees two already-labelled neighbours with *different* labels?
A:: Inherit one of them, and record an equivalence between the two labels in a union-find structure. After pass 1, equivalence classes are collapsed to canonical labels and pass 2 relabels every pixel.

Q:: Why is Union-Find the right data structure for the equivalence step in two-pass labelling?
A:: It supports near-constant-time union and find, so each merge is O(α(n)) and resolving all classes after pass 1 is essentially linear.

Q:: Define area and perimeter for a binary connected component, and give one issue with the simple grid-count perimeter.
A:: Area = pixel count. Grid-count perimeter: for each foreground pixel, count its missing 4-neighbours. The grid count overestimates perimeter on rotated shapes, biasing compactness measurements; marching squares is more accurate.

Q:: What is the convex hull of an object, and why is it useful?
A:: The smallest convex polygon containing every foreground pixel. It defines a reference convex shape, used for **solidity** (object area divided by hull area) — a measure of how concave / fragmented an object is.

Q:: Write the compactness (circularity) formula and its value for a perfect circle.
A:: $f_\text{circ} = 4\pi\,\text{Area}/\text{Perimeter}^2$. Equals 1 for a perfect circle; falls toward 0 as shapes elongate or become irregular.

Q:: Define solidity, and give the value for any convex shape.
A:: $\text{solidity} = \text{Area} / \text{Area of convex hull}$. Equal to 1 for any convex shape (rectangle, circle, triangle); falls below 1 for objects with concavities or holes.

Q:: Why might you want both compactness and solidity instead of just one?
A:: Compactness measures roundness vs. elongation; solidity measures convexity vs. concavity. A long thin convex bar has low compactness but solidity 1; a horse silhouette has many concavities, so even though it's not too elongated, solidity is well below 1. They probe different aspects of shape.

Q:: How do you compute the centre of mass of a binary connected component?
A:: Average the $(x, y)$ coordinates of all foreground pixels: $\mu = (\frac{1}{N}\sum x_i, \frac{1}{N}\sum y_i)$.

Q:: Write the second-moment matrix of a binary object and explain what its eigenvectors represent.
A:: $M = \begin{pmatrix}\mu_{20} & \mu_{11}\\ \mu_{11} & \mu_{02}\end{pmatrix}$ where $\mu_{20}, \mu_{02}, \mu_{11}$ are central second moments of pixel coordinates. Its eigenvectors are the principal axes of the object — the largest gives the orientation (axis of least second moment), the smaller gives the perpendicular direction.

Q:: Closed-form formula for the orientation $\theta$ of a binary object.
A:: $\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{atan2}(2\mu_{11},\, \mu_{20} - \mu_{02})$.

Q:: How is eccentricity computed from the eigenvalues of $M$, and what does it mean intuitively?
A:: $e = \sqrt{1 - \lambda_2/\lambda_1}$ with $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2$. 0 for a circle (equal eigenvalues), approaches 1 for very elongated shapes.

Q:: Image moments and the structure tensor (lecture 12) compute the same thing on different inputs. Name the inputs.
A:: Image moments: pixel coordinates $(x, y)$ of an object. Structure tensor: gradient values $(I_x, I_y)$ inside a window. Both are 2×2 covariance matrices whose eigendecomposition gives orientation and aspect.

Q:: Why is morphological opening (erode then dilate) useful before connected-components labelling?
A:: It removes small foreground specks that would otherwise become spurious tiny components, so downstream area/perimeter statistics aren't polluted by noise.

content.newText:
--- tags: [lecture] course: Computer-Vision source: 14_intro_to_pytorch_DL(1).pdf lecture: 14 created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 14 — Intro to PyTorch (foundations for DL) ## Overview Pivot lecture: from classical algorithmic vision to deep learning. The lecture sets up the **mathematical and software toolbox** the rest of the course will use. Three threads: 1. **Tensors** — the universal data type. Shapes, slicing, reshape/permute/contiguous, devices (CPU/GPU), dtype, NumPy interop. 2. **Linear algebra primitives** — element-wise ops, broadcasting, dot products, matrix multiplications, the einsum shortcut. 3. **Calculus and gradient descent** — the derivative as slope, the gradient $\nabla f$ as direction of fastest growth, gradient descent as the iterative rule, and PyTorch's **autograd** that computes gradients for you. ## Key concepts - [[pytorch-tensor]] — N-D arrays in PyTorch, shape / dtype / device - [[broadcasting]] — rules for combining tensors of mismatched shape - [[dot-product]] — vector dot, cosine similarity, the linear-model heart - [[matrix-multiplication]] — batched linear layers $y = X W^\top + b$ - [[einsum]] — Einstein summation for arbitrary tensor contractions - [[gradient-descent]] — $x^{t+1} = x^t - \lambda \nabla f$ - [[pytorch-autograd]] — `requires_grad`, `backward()`, optimizers ## Equations Dot product (slide 25): $$\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b} = \sum_i a_i b_i = \mathbf{a}^\top \mathbf{b} = \|\mathbf{a}\|\,\|\mathbf{b}\|\cos\theta$$ Vector norm: $$\|\mathbf{x}\| = \sqrt{\mathbf{x}\cdot\mathbf{x}}$$ Cosine similarity (slide 26): $$S_C(A, B) = \cos\theta = \frac{\mathbf{A}\cdot\mathbf{B}}{\|\mathbf{A}\|\,\|\mathbf{B}\|}$$ Linear model (slide 28): $$f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_i w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}\cdot\mathbf{x} + b$$ Batched: $Y = X W^\top + b$ with $X \in \mathbb{R}^{B\times n_\text{in}}$, $W \in \mathbb{R}^{n_\text{out}\times n_\text{in}}$. Derivative (slide 33): $$f'(x) = \lim_{h\to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$$ N-D gradient (slide 35): $$\nabla f = \left[\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \dots, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}\right]$$ Gradient-descent update (slides 34, 36): $$x^{t+1} = x^t - \lambda\, \frac{df}{dx} \quad\text{(scalar)}, \qquad \mathbf{x}^{t+1} = \mathbf{x}^t - \lambda\, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^t) \quad\text{(N-D)}$$ ## Diagrams ### Tensor mental model ```mermaid flowchart LR s["d=0: scalar
torch.zeros(()) or torch.zeros(1)"] --> v["d=1: vector
torch.zeros(3)"] v --> m["d=2: matrix
torch.zeros((4,4))"] m --> c["d=3: third-order tensor
torch.zeros((3,4,4))"] c --> n["d=N: image batch
(B, C, H, W)"] ``` ### Strides + storage view of `view`/`reshape`/`permute` ```mermaid flowchart TD storage["1-D underlying storage
[0,1,2,…,15]"] --> meta1["x.shape=(4,4)
strides=(4,1)"] meta1 -->|"x.view((2,8))"| v["new shape (2,8)
strides (8,1) — same storage"] meta1 -->|"x.permute(1,0) / x.T"| p["new shape (4,4)
strides=(1,4) — same storage
not contiguous"] p -->|"y.contiguous()"| copy["new storage with
strides=(4,1)"] ``` `view` requires a contiguous buffer. After `permute`, call `contiguous()` before `.view()` (or use `reshape`, which copies if needed). ### Broadcasting decision tree (slide 15) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["operand shapes a, b"] --> pad["pad shorter shape
with 1s on the LEFT"] pad --> rt["compare dim-by-dim
from the RIGHT"] rt --> q{"each pair: equal,
or one is 1?"} q -->|"yes"| stretch["dim of size 1 is
'stretched' to match"] q -->|"no"| err["RuntimeError
(e.g. (3,2) vs (3,3))"] stretch --> ok["element-wise op proceeds"] ``` ### Linear model in tensor form ```mermaid flowchart LR xb["x_batch (B, n_in)"] --> mm["@ W^T (n_in, n_out)"] W["W (n_out, n_in)"] -->|"transpose"| mm mm --> add["+ b (1, n_out) — broadcast"] b["bias (n_out,)"] --> add add --> y["y (B, n_out)"] ``` ### Gradient descent in 1D vs N-D ```mermaid flowchart LR x0["start x_0"] --> g["compute ∇f(x)"] g --> step["x ← x − λ·∇f"] step --> chk{"converged?"} chk -->|"no"| g chk -->|"yes"| done["local min x*"] ``` | Setting | Update | |---|---| | 1D ($f: \mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$) | $x \leftarrow x - \lambda f'(x)$ | | N-D ($f: \mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$) | $\mathbf{x} \leftarrow \mathbf{x} - \lambda \nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ | ### PyTorch autograd lifecycle (slide 38, 41–42) ```mermaid flowchart TD create["x = x0.clone().requires_grad_(True)"] --> fwd["fx = f(x)
(builds computation graph)"] fwd --> bwd["fx.backward()
(populates x.grad)"] bwd --> upd["with torch.no_grad():
x -= lr * x.grad"] upd --> zero["x.grad.zero_()
(reset for next step)"] zero --> fwd ``` With an optimizer: ```mermaid flowchart TD init["params = ...; optim = SGD(params, lr)"] --> loop["each iteration:"] loop --> z["optim.zero_grad()"] --> f["fx = f(params)"] --> b["fx.backward()"] --> s["optim.step()"] s --> loop ``` ## Watch-outs (from the slides) - **dtype defaults differ**: NumPy float = float64; PyTorch float = float32. Casting copies. - **In-place ops** (`x.log_()`, `x += 1`) overwrite `x` and can break autograd. - **`view` vs `reshape`**: `view` requires contiguous storage; `reshape` may copy. - **After `permute` / `.T`** the tensor is not contiguous → `.contiguous()` before `.view()`. - **`numel` is conserved** by view/reshape — element count in must equal element count out. - **Devices**: `x.to("cuda")` returns a new tensor on the GPU (it does *not* mutate `x`). ## Open questions - When does the batched `(B, n_in) @ W.T` formulation outperform `einsum("bi,oi->bo", X, W)`? They should compile to the same kernel, but in practice… - For non-convex losses, does momentum / Adam buy you faster convergence or different minima? (Lecture stops at vanilla SGD.) ## Connections to other lectures - The **gradient** here is the same mathematical object as the [[image-gradient]] from lecture 11 — but applied to a scalar loss over network parameters instead of intensity over pixels. - The covariance/eigendecomposition machinery from [[structure-tensor]] / [[image-moments]] reappears as PCA in this lecture's broader context. - This lecture's machinery is the foundation for everything from [[gradient-descent]] training of CNNs (lecture 16) onward.

--- tags: [lecture] course: Computer-Vision source: 14_intro_to_pytorch_DL(1).pdf lecture: 14 created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 14 — Intro to PyTorch (foundations for DL) ## Overview Pivot lecture: from classical algorithmic vision to deep learning. The lecture sets up the **mathematical and software toolbox** the rest of the course will use. Three threads: 1. **Tensors** — the universal data type. Shapes, slicing, reshape/permute/contiguous, devices (CPU/GPU), dtype, NumPy interop. 2. **Linear algebra primitives** — element-wise ops, broadcasting, dot products, matrix multiplications, the einsum shortcut. 3. **Calculus and gradient descent** — the derivative as slope, the gradient $\nabla f$ as direction of fastest growth, gradient descent as the iterative rule, and PyTorch's **autograd** that computes gradients for you. ## Key concepts - [[pytorch-tensor]] — N-D arrays in PyTorch, shape / dtype / device - [[broadcasting]] — rules for combining tensors of mismatched shape - [[dot-product]] — vector dot, cosine similarity, the linear-model heart - [[matrix-multiplication]] — batched linear layers $y = X W^\top + b$ - [[einsum]] — Einstein summation for arbitrary tensor contractions - [[gradient-descent]] — $x^{t+1} = x^t - \lambda \nabla f$ - [[pytorch-autograd]] — `requires_grad`, `backward()`, optimizers ## Equations Dot product (slide 25): $$\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b} = \sum_i a_i b_i = \mathbf{a}^\top \mathbf{b} = \|\mathbf{a}\|\,\|\mathbf{b}\|\cos\theta$$ Vector norm: $$\|\mathbf{x}\| = \sqrt{\mathbf{x}\cdot\mathbf{x}}$$ Cosine similarity (slide 26): $$S_C(A, B) = \cos\theta = \frac{\mathbf{A}\cdot\mathbf{B}}{\|\mathbf{A}\|\,\|\mathbf{B}\|}$$ Linear model (slide 28): $$f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_i w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}\cdot\mathbf{x} + b$$ Batched: $Y = X W^\top + b$ with $X \in \mathbb{R}^{B\times n_\text{in}}$, $W \in \mathbb{R}^{n_\text{out}\times n_\text{in}}$. Derivative (slide 33): $$f'(x) = \lim_{h\to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$$ N-D gradient (slide 35): $$\nabla f = \left[\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \dots, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}\right]$$ Gradient-descent update (slides 34, 36): $$x^{t+1} = x^t - \lambda\, \frac{df}{dx} \quad\text{(scalar)}, \qquad \mathbf{x}^{t+1} = \mathbf{x}^t - \lambda\, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^t) \quad\text{(N-D)}$$ ## Diagrams ### Tensor mental model ```mermaid flowchart LR s["d=0: scalar
torch.zeros(()) or torch.zeros(1)"] --> v["d=1: vector
torch.zeros(3)"] v --> m["d=2: matrix
torch.zeros((4,4))"] m --> c["d=3: third-order tensor
torch.zeros((3,4,4))"] c --> n["d=N: image batch
(B, C, H, W)"] ``` ### Strides + storage view of `view`/`reshape`/`permute` ```mermaid flowchart TD storage["1-D underlying storage
[0,1,2,…,15]"] --> meta1["x.shape=(4,4)
strides=(4,1)"] meta1 -->|"x.view((2,8))"| v["new shape (2,8)
strides (8,1) — same storage"] meta1 -->|"x.permute(1,0) / x.T"| p["new shape (4,4)
strides=(1,4) — same storage
not contiguous"] p -->|"y.contiguous()"| copy["new storage with
strides=(4,1)"] ``` `view` requires a contiguous buffer. After `permute`, call `contiguous()` before `.view()` (or use `reshape`, which copies if needed). ### Broadcasting decision tree (slide 15) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["operand shapes a, b"] --> pad["pad shorter shape
with 1s on the LEFT"] pad --> rt["compare dim-by-dim
from the RIGHT"] rt --> q{"each pair: equal,
or one is 1?"} q -->|"yes"| stretch["dim of size 1 is
'stretched' to match"] q -->|"no"| err["RuntimeError
(e.g. (3,2) vs (3,3))"] stretch --> ok["element-wise op proceeds"] ``` ### Linear model in tensor form ```mermaid flowchart LR xb["x_batch (B, n_in)"] --> mm["@ W^T (n_in, n_out)"] W["W (n_out, n_in)"] -->|"transpose"| mm mm --> add["+ b (1, n_out) — broadcast"] b["bias (n_out,)"] --> add add --> y["y (B, n_out)"] ``` ### Gradient descent in 1D vs N-D ```mermaid flowchart LR x0["start x_0"] --> g["compute ∇f(x)"] g --> step["x ← x − λ·∇f"] step --> chk{"converged?"} chk -->|"no"| g chk -->|"yes"| done["local min x*"] ``` | Setting | Update | |---|---| | 1D ($f: \mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$) | $x \leftarrow x - \lambda f'(x)$ | | N-D ($f: \mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$) | $\mathbf{x} \leftarrow \mathbf{x} - \lambda \nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ | ### PyTorch autograd lifecycle (slide 38, 41–42) ```mermaid flowchart TD create["x = x0.clone().requires_grad_(True)"] --> fwd["fx = f(x)
(builds computation graph)"] fwd --> bwd["fx.backward()
(populates x.grad)"] bwd --> upd["with torch.no_grad():
x -= lr * x.grad"] upd --> zero["x.grad.zero_()
(reset for next step)"] zero --> fwd ``` With an optimizer: ```mermaid flowchart TD init["params = ...; optim = SGD(params, lr)"] --> loop["each iteration:"] loop --> z["optim.zero_grad()"] --> f["fx = f(params)"] --> b["fx.backward()"] --> s["optim.step()"] s --> loop ``` ## Watch-outs (from the slides) - **dtype defaults differ**: NumPy float = float64; PyTorch float = float32. content.newText:
---
name: PyTorch tensor
description: N-dimensional array with shape, dtype, and device — the universal data type in PyTorch
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [14_intro_to_pytorch_DL(1).pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, pytorch, tensor]
---

# PyTorch tensor

The **tensor** is PyTorch's universal data type — an N-dimensional array with three pieces of metadata: **shape**, **dtype**, and **device**.

## Construction

```python
torch.zeros(3)            # vector of zeros, shape (3,)
torch.zeros((4, 4))       # 4×4 matrix
torch.zeros((3, 4, 4))    # 3 × 4×4 matrices
torch.empty((4, 4))       # uninitialized (garbage memory)
torch.eye(3)              # identity
torch.arange(5)           # [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]
torch.ones(...)
torch.rand((4, 4))        # uniform [0, 1)
torch.randn((4, 4))       # normal(0, 1)
```

## NumPy interop

```python
x_np = x_torch.numpy()                  # tensor → ndarray (shares memory if on CPU)
y_t  = torch.from_numpy(y_np)           # ndarray → tensor (shares memory)
y_t  = torch.as_tensor(y_np, dtype=torch.float32)
x.item()                                # 0-D tensor → Python scalar
```

dtype default differs:
- NumPy float arrays default to **float64**.
- PyTorch float tensors default to **float32**.

Casting (e.g. `x.to(torch.float64)`) creates a copy.

## Shape primitives

`numel()` is the total number of elements; it must be conserved by `view` / `reshape`.

| Operation | What it does | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `x.view(s)` | reinterpret as shape `s` | requires **contiguous** storage |
| `x.reshape(s)` | reshape, copy if needed | always works |
| `x.permute(...)` | reorder dimensions | not contiguous afterwards |
| `x.T` | shorthand for permute on a 2-D tensor | same caveat |
| `x.contiguous()` | force a contiguous copy | call before `.view()` if you've permuted |
| `-1` in shape | infer that dim from `numel` | only one allowed |

Image-pipeline gotcha: NumPy arrays often come as `(L, L, 3)` (HWC) but PyTorch convolutions expect `(3, L, L)` (CHW) — fix with `permute(2, 0, 1)`.

## Element-wise ops

Unary: `x.log()`, `x.sin()`, `x.cos()`, `x.exp()`. Trailing-underscore versions (`x.log_()`) are **in-place** and overwrite `x` — they can break autograd if `x` is part of a computation graph.

Binary: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, logical ops. Operand shapes must be compatible — see [[broadcasting]].

## Aggregation (reductions)

```python
x.sum()                # scalar
x.sum(dim=0)           # collapse dim 0; output ndim = x.ndim - 1
x.sum(dim=1, keepdim=True)
x.mean(...), x.std(...), x.min(...), x.max(...)
```

A reduction over `dim=k` removes that dimension from the output (or keeps it as size 1 with `keepdim=True`). Useful pattern: averaging along the last dim of a batch.

## Devices

```python
x = torch.zeros(3, device="cuda")     # create directly on GPU
x = x.to("cuda")                      # copy to GPU (does NOT mutate x; returns a new tensor)
x = x.to("cuda:1")                    # specific GPU
```

## Related

- [[broadcasting]] — shape compatibility rules
- [[dot-product]], [[matrix-multiplication]] — the operations we want fast on tensors
- [[pytorch-autograd]] — what makes tensors differentiable

content.newText:
---
name: Broadcasting
description: Implicit virtual replication of size-1 dimensions to make differently-shaped tensors compatible for element-wise ops
type: concept
courses: [Computer-Vision]
source: [14_intro_to_pytorch_DL(1).pdf]
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, pytorch, numpy, tensor]
---

# Broadcasting

Element-wise operations between tensors require shape compatibility. **Broadcasting** is the rule that makes mismatched shapes work without an explicit copy.

## Rules (slide 15)

1. **Right-align the shapes** (compare from the trailing dimension).
2. **Pad the shorter shape** on the left with 1s until it has the same number of dims.
3. For each dimension pair, they must be **equal** *or* **one of them must be 1**.
4. The dim of size 1 is **virtually stretched** (no actual copy in memory) to match the other.
5. Otherwise: `RuntimeError`.

## Examples

```python
torch.rand(3, 2) + torch.rand(3, 1)   # OK → (3, 2)
torch.rand(3, 2) + torch.rand(3, 3)   # ERROR (2 vs 3, neither is 1)
torch.rand(4, 3) + torch.rand(3,)     # OK after right-pad to (1, 3) → (4, 3)
torch.rand(4, 3) + torch.rand(4,)     # ERROR — trailing dims 3 vs 4
torch.rand(4, 3) + torch.rand(4, 1)   # OK → (4, 3)
torch.rand(10, 3, 4) + torch.rand(4,) # OK → (10, 3, 4)
```

## When you need to reshape

Sometimes the tensor you have is the right *size* but in the wrong *position*:

```python
A = torch.rand(4, 3)
b = torch.rand(4)              # add to each ROW won't work directly
A + b                          # ERROR — pads to (1, 4) on the left, dim mismatch with 3
A + b.reshape(4, 1)            # OK → broadcasts column → all columns get b_i
```

## Why we use it

Broadcasting replaces the explicit `for i,j: ...` Python loops with vectorized C/CUDA kernels. It's the reason you can write a per-row bias addition or normalization in one line without sacrificing speed.

## Common patterns in DL

- **Bias addition** in a linear layer: `(B, n_out) + (n_out,)` broadcasts over the batch dim.
- **Normalization**: `(B, C) - mean.reshape(1, C)` then divide.
- **Attention masks**: `(B, T) * mask.reshape(1, T)`.

## Related

- [[pytorch-tensor]] — the underlying object
- [[dot-product]], [[matrix-multiplication]] — operations that often follow a broadcast
- [[einsum]] — generalises broadcast + reduce
